briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. We will now commence the climate briefing for April 19, 2026.

sections.title: ⚠️ The Reality of Domestic Climate Change Response and Retreat in Public Awareness
sections.content: Recently, concerns have been raised in South Korea regarding a lukewarm attitude towards climate change response and a retreat in public awareness. The Bank of Korea has shown a passive stance, implementing only 2 out of 10 climate change response tasks over the past four years [1]. Reports have also indicated a 5%p decrease in the proportion of people recognizing the severity of climate change within a year [3], or a diminished perception of its seriousness [18, 63]. Furthermore, criticisms have emerged that the focus in expanding renewable energy is more on 'packaging' than on substantial details [51]. There have also been calls to remove misleading labels like 'CFE' and genuinely confront the climate crisis [28]. Some voices have even questioned the necessity of a Ministry of Climate Change [69].

sections.title: 🔴 Intensification of the Global Climate Crisis and Its Direct Impacts
sections.content: Globally, the severity of the climate crisis is intensifying, directly impacting humanity and the natural environment. The Sahel region is experiencing tragedy due to climate change-induced droughts [26], and severe weather events such as tornadoes, floods, and hail have struck the US Midwest, amplifying climate change concerns [30]. Notably, the Lahaina area of Maui, Hawaii, suffered extensive damage from its worst floods in 20 years [74], and a site in Sydney, Australia, is also at severe risk of flooding [73]. Despite water conservation efforts, the impacts of climate change are outpacing them in areas like Phoenix, Denver, and Las Vegas [61, 66]. Reports that climate change threatens 2,500-year-old shipwrecks off the coast of Gibraltar and poses a disaster for mental health demonstrate the widespread nature of this crisis [36, 65].

sections.title: 🌱 Expanding Citizen Participation and Carbon Neutrality Practices through 'Climate Change Week'
sections.content: Amidst this crisis, efforts are actively underway both domestically and internationally to encourage citizens' carbon neutrality practices by observing 'Climate Change Week' in commemoration of 'Earth Day.' The Ministry of Climate launched '2026 Climate Change Week,' emphasizing the message 'Earth is Undergoing a Green Transformation' [9, 12, 15, 25, 49]. Various local governments, including Jinju City, Busan City, Ulsan City, Daejeon City, and Incheon City, have organized diverse citizen participation events and campaigns [2, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 13, 14, 16, 17, 19, 20, 21, 27, 31, 33, 34, 47]. Notably, Ulsan City and Daejeon City expanded citizen participation in carbon neutrality practices through 'nationwide simultaneous lights-out' events [7, 21, 23]. Private organizations such as the Veterans Health Service Medical Corporation and Shincheonji Volunteer Association also joined carbon neutrality campaigns [38, 48]. Gyeongsan City is strengthening its practical approach by expanding experience-based education through upcycling instead of theoretical learning [41].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic and International Efforts and Challenges for Achieving Carbon Neutrality Goals
sections.content: Domestic and international efforts to achieve carbon neutrality goals are ongoing, but they simultaneously face various challenges. South Korea plans to invest 3.4 trillion won in carbon-neutral technologies in 2026, a 14.1% increase from the previous year [46]. Cheonan City has successfully reduced 110,000 tons of greenhouse gas, and Korea Southern Power has raised its greenhouse gas reduction targets, demonstrating continuous concrete achievements and efforts [39, 44]. Yoon Seok-dae, CEO of Korea Water Resources Corporation, was appointed as a special advisor to the Philippine Climate Change Commission, contributing to international cooperation [4, 22, 24, 29, 32, 35]. However, the grand transition to renewable energy is encountering 'reefs,' and with energy security warning lights on, there is active discussion on whether renewable energy transition will be a breakthrough [52, 53]. Wind energy is also described as a 'flickering hope,' indicating that it faces significant challenges [55].

sections.title: 🌍 International Research and Adaptation Trends Regarding Climate Change
sections.content: The international community is conducting various research and activities to understand and adapt to the impacts of climate change. Research on ancient seabird guano has revealed the potential effects of climate change on future populations [60], and studies have also been published indicating that one of the world's rarest mice is adapting to climate change [68]. Scientific analyses are also being conducted on potential phenomena that could occur when global warming reaches 2 degrees Celsius and its impact on sea breezes [62, 70]. Meanwhile, data from Kyoto, Japan, spanning 1,200 years since the 9th century, confirms that cherry blossoms are blooming earlier, providing visible evidence of climate change [72]. Efforts for citizen engagement are also continuing, such as 'tUrn Week' events aimed at raising climate change awareness on campuses and sold-out film screenings that spark climate change conversations [59, 71].

briefing.closing: This concludes today's climate briefing. We once again emphasize the importance of continued attention and effort in responding to the climate crisis, and with that, we conclude today's briefing.

articles.title: The Guardian view on Japan’s cherry blossom: when spring slips out of time | Editorial
articles.description: A 1,200-year dataset shows the ‘peak bloom’ is arriving earlier. Global heating is unsettling nature’s rhythms – and their cultural meaningA picture posted on social media last April by Prof Yasuyuki Aono of a spreadsheet, with its blank row for 2026, carries a quiet poignancy. Prof Aono died before he got to fill in this year’s entry for when the cherry blossom fully bloomed in Kyoto. The academic had spent decades reconstructing dates of flowering that go back to the ninth century. His work il

summary.phenomenon: The 'peak bloom' period for cherry blossoms in Kyoto, Japan, is arriving earlier. This phenomenon has been confirmed through a 1,200-year dataset reconstructed from the 9th century.
summary.cause: This is due to global heating disrupting nature's rhythms.
summary.outlook: These changes are projected to affect not only nature's rhythms but also the cultural significance of cherry blossoms.

articles.title: Maui residents are rebuilding Lahaina for locals, not tourists: ‘In Hawaii, we take care of one another’
articles.description: After deadly 2023 fires, recent storms and ICE raids, Lahaina residents are determined to rebuild the town for their communityIn March, Hawaii was hit with two back-to-back storms, bringing the worst flooding it’s seen in 20 years. In Lahaina, Maui, muddy flood waters turned streets into rivers and carved new paths through the barren landscape, breaking open roads and flooding houses. In their wake, sinkholes appeared, engulfing cars.This is nearly three years after the deadliest wildfires in US

summary.phenomenon: In March 2024, two consecutive storms caused the worst flooding in 20 years in Lahaina, Maui, Hawaii. This resulted in muddy floodwaters turning streets into rivers, destroying roads, inundating homes, and creating sinkholes that engulfed vehicles.
summary.cause: Due to the occurrence of two consecutive storms.
summary.outlook: Lahaina residents are expected to dedicate themselves to rebuilding the town for their community, focusing on local residents rather than tourists.

articles.original_description: After deadly 2023 fires, recent storms and ICE raids, Lahaina residents are determined to rebuild the town for their communityIn March, Hawaii was hit with two back-to-back storms, bringing the worst flooding it’s seen in 20 years. In Lahaina, Maui, muddy flood waters turned streets into rivers and carved new paths through the barren landscape, breaking open roads and flooding houses. In their wake, sinkholes appeared, engulfing cars.This is nearly three years after the deadliest wildfires in US
